name: Raghib Khan
learn: I think I needed to voice my concerns more with my group. Because I came late and had to read the case pretty rapidly, I felt as though I had to agree with the line of reasoning of my group members as I didn't have enough time to read all the facts. Despite not feeling too confident with the "race" decision, I went along with the group's stance.

name: amy
learn: i learned that it’s very important to consider all opinions in the room. it’s important to listen to everyone’s opinion and not let one’s thoughts overrule another’s. i saw that through the case study, even just one persons thoughts can be so powerful and end up making or breaking the situation, and if that one person is overlooked, a lot can go wrong, and in the case of the study, lives were taken away.

name: Zara Zhang
learn: The idea about groupthink and conformity is really interesting that collaboration and communication is not always improving the final decision, when there is exogenous variables such as the time limit or budget.

name: Michelle Beilis
learn: This lesson reinforced the importance of consider the flip side. Not only looking at the failures but seeing how the successes compare in contrast. Instead of just proving why you shouldn’t launch, engineers had to prove why not to launch. Exploring both sides with equal effort can enable people to see the full picture and make a fully informed decision, understanding all the risks.

name: Jacob Freedman
learn: I learned that it's important in group settings to consider all perspectives and not focus too much on certain data points.

name: Matthias
learn: I think the lesson really helped with putting some perspective into how we should approach different key decisions and how we should evaluate the information given to us. I think the linkage to the challenger situation helps sends the message home where ultimately we will definetly face these situations in real life.

name: Angela Bu
learn: I learned that regardless of how much CORRECT, ACCURATE, AND RELIABLE data someone has, it can all be dismissed by someone in power. It only takes a couple (or even one person) that has influence to say the data is inconclusive or to question the research credibility for others to follow suit. You need more than just data, you need influence, power, public speaking skills, and you need courage.

name: Anyela
learn: What I learned in today’s lecture was to challenge myself and my group and look at the bigger picture when analyzing a problem instead of just working with the data provided to us. I also learned that if I disagree with somebody on a project in the future, that I should share my opinion with some proof to my word. If I have evidence, it will make my statement stronger and perhaps convince the other people to take my side.

name: Neveille Patel
learn: When making decisions we need to have a structured process in order to make informed calls. I should use a model such as rational analysis model where I incorporate every single piece of information possible in order to make a logical and accurate decision.

name: Natiela Alemu
learn: The idea that having all the expertise, data and knowledge in the world will mean nothing if a spirit of open discussion doesn't exist at the centre of any decision making process, especially when so much is at stake.
